enrichment.short_description: Affirm is a publicly traded fintech (NASDAQ: AFRM) that lets shoppers split purchases into interest-bearing or 0 percent installment loans at checkout, while participating merchants pay a transaction fee to offer the option.
enrichment.seo_title: Affirm Review 2026: How the BNPL Leader Works
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Affirm review for 2026: how the buy now pay later leader works for shoppers and merchants, its fees, APR ranges, and how it compares to Klarna and Afterpay.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Affirm is a San Francisco-based financial technology company that pioneered a major slice of the buy now pay later (BNPL) industry in the United States. It was founded in 2012 by PayPal co-founder Max Levchin, alongside Nathan Gettings, Jeffrey Kaditz, and Alex Rampell, with the goal of offering consumers a transparent, interest-honest alternative to revolving credit card debt. Affirm went public on Nasdaq under the ticker AFRM on January 13, 2021, raising roughly 1.2 billion dollars in its IPO, and it remains one of the few large BNPL providers to trade as a standalone public company rather than being folded into a bank or larger payments group. As of mid-2026 the company reports tens of millions of active consumers, more than 500,000 active merchants, and tens of billions of dollars in annual gross merchandise volume processed through its network. Affirm's core product lets shoppers split a purchase into payments at the point of sale, either through Pay in 4 (four interest-free biweekly installments) or longer monthly installment loans ranging from about three to sixty months with APRs from 0 percent up to 36 percent depending on the borrower and merchant. Affirm does not lend directly; it partners with issuing banks such as Cross River Bank, Evolve Bank and Trust, and Celtic Bank to originate the loans, then uses proprietary machine learning underwriting to approve or decline each transaction in real time, looking beyond a traditional FICO score. The company's revenue comes from two sources: fees charged to merchants (which can run up to roughly 12.5 percent of transaction value in exchange for boosted conversion and average order value) and interest paid by consumers on interest-bearing plans. Affirm markets itself on the promise of no late fees and no compounding interest, and it shows the total repayment cost upfront before a purchase is confirmed. It integrates directly with major ecommerce platforms including Shopify, Magento, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, and Salesforce Commerce Cloud, and it has a notable partnership with Amazon that surfaces Affirm as a payment option at checkout. Shoppers can also use a virtual card to pay with Affirm at retailers without a native integration, and the Affirm Card extends the model into everyday in-store spending as a debit card with an optional pay-over-time feature. Affirm's main competitors are Klarna and Block-owned Afterpay; relative to those two, Affirm leans more heavily on larger-ticket, longer-term installment loans with interest rather than exclusively short, interest-free four-payment plans, and it has historically positioned itself as the more credit-transparent, no-late-fee option in the category.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Affirm
paragraphs: Affirm is a buy now pay later (BNPL) fintech company founded in 2012 by Max Levchin, the PayPal co-founder, along with Nathan Gettings, Jeffrey Kaditz, and Alex Rampell. Headquartered in San Francisco, the company lets online and in-store shoppers split purchases into installment payments at checkout instead of paying the full amount upfront or opening a traditional credit card.

Affirm went public on Nasdaq (ticker AFRM) on January 13, 2021, raising approximately 1.2 billion dollars in its IPO. It is one of the largest publicly traded BNPL companies, reporting tens of millions of active consumers and hundreds of thousands of active merchants as of 2026, with tens of billions of dollars processed annually.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Affirm's headline products are Pay in 4, which splits a purchase into four interest-free biweekly payments, and longer monthly installment loans that stretch from about three to sixty months with APRs ranging from 0 percent to 36 percent depending on the purchase and the borrower's approval terms.

Affirm does not act as a direct lender; instead it partners with issuing banks like Cross River Bank, Evolve Bank and Trust, and Celtic Bank to originate loans, and it uses machine learning underwriting to approve or decline transactions instantly. Shoppers can use Affirm at directly integrated merchants (Shopify, Amazon, Walmart historically, and thousands of other stores) or via a virtual card at retailers without a native integration. The Affirm Card extends the model to everyday debit spending with optional pay-over-time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: For consumers, Affirm charges no application fees, no late fees, and no compounding interest; the total repayment amount is shown before the purchase is confirmed, and interest, where charged, ranges from 0 percent to 36 percent APR depending on the loan.

For merchants, Affirm charges a transaction fee that can reach roughly 12.5 percent of the purchase amount in exchange for higher checkout conversion, larger average order values, and instant upfront payment, since Affirm pays the merchant in full at the time of sale and assumes the repayment risk.

features.description: Splits eligible purchases into four equal, interest-free payments made every two weeks.

features.title: Monthly installment loans
features.description: Longer-term financing from about three to sixty months with fixed APRs from 0 percent to 36 percent.

features.title: Affirm Card
features.description: A hybrid debit and BNPL card that lets cardholders choose to pay now or split eligible purchases over time.

features.title: Virtual card checkout
features.description: Generates a one-time card number so shoppers can use Affirm at merchants without a direct integration.

features.title: No late fees
features.description: Affirm does not charge late fees or compound interest, though missed payments can still affect credit standing.

features.title: Machine learning underwriting
features.description: Real-time approval decisions that weigh more than a traditional credit score to assess borrower risk.

features.title: Merchant integrations
features.description: Native support for Shopify, Magento, WooCommerce, BigCommerce, Salesforce Commerce Cloud, and a partnership with Amazon.

features.title: Adaptive Checkout
features.description: A merchant-facing tool that surfaces the most relevant Affirm payment terms for each shopper at checkout.

pros: No late fees are ever charged to consumers
0 percent APR is available on many qualifying purchases
Wide merchant acceptance, including Amazon and thousands of Shopify stores
Total repayment cost is shown transparently before purchase
Publicly traded and regulated, which adds a layer of financial transparency
cons: Interest rates can reach 36 percent APR on some loans, higher than many credit card promotional rates
Missed payments can be reported to credit bureaus and affect credit scores
Not accepted at every merchant despite the virtual card workaround
Merchant fees of up to roughly 12.5 percent can be costly for smaller businesses
Approval and available terms vary by purchase, so financing is not guaranteed or consistent

faqs.question: Is Affirm a bank?
faqs.answer: No. Affirm partners with issuing banks such as Cross River Bank, Evolve Bank and Trust, and Celtic Bank to originate its loans.

faqs.question: Does Affirm charge late fees?
faqs.answer: No, Affirm does not charge late fees, though missed payments can still be reported and affect a borrower's credit.

faqs.question: What is the Affirm Card?
faqs.answer: A debit card issued by Affirm that lets holders pay immediately from their bank balance or choose to pay over time on eligible purchases.

faqs.question: Is Affirm a public company?
faqs.answer: Yes, Affirm has traded on Nasdaq under the ticker AFRM since its IPO on January 13, 2021.

faqs.question: Who founded Affirm?
faqs.answer: Max Levchin, the PayPal co-founder, founded Affirm in 2012 along with Nathan Gettings, Jeffrey Kaditz, and Alex Rampell.

faqs.question: How does Affirm make money?
faqs.answer: Affirm earns revenue from merchant transaction fees and from interest charged to consumers on interest-bearing installment loans.

faqs.question: How is Affirm different from Klarna and Afterpay?
faqs.answer: Affirm leans more heavily on longer-term, sometimes interest-bearing installment loans, while Klarna and Afterpay have historically emphasized short, interest-free four-payment plans, though all three now offer overlapping products.

faqs.question: Does using Affirm affect my credit score?
faqs.answer: It can. Longer-term Affirm loans may involve a credit check, and payment history can be reported to credit bureaus, which can help or hurt a score depending on repayment behavior.

enrichment.short_description: AfterShip is an ecommerce post-purchase platform that gives online stores branded shipment tracking pages, automated delivery notifications, and returns management across more than a thousand carriers worldwide.
enrichment.seo_title: AfterShip Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Setup
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AfterShip review for 2026 covering shipment tracking pricing tiers, branded tracking pages, returns management, Shopify integration, and how it reduces WISMO tickets.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AfterShip is an ecommerce post-purchase software company founded in 2012, originally based in Hong Kong and now headquartered in Singapore, with additional offices across North America, Europe, and Asia Pacific. The company built its reputation on shipment tracking: it aggregates delivery data from more than 1,100 carriers worldwide into a single branded tracking page and notification system that online stores embed on their own domain, so customers can check order status without leaving the retailer's site. Over time AfterShip expanded from a single tracking tool into a broader post-purchase suite that includes AfterShip Tracking, AfterShip Returns (a self-service returns and exchanges center), and AfterShip Personalization (AI-driven product recommendations shown on tracking pages and in notification emails). The platform is especially popular with Shopify and Shopify Plus merchants, where it is offered as a native app, but it also integrates with WooCommerce, Magento, BigCommerce, and custom stores through an open API. Core use cases include reducing WISMO (where is my order) support tickets through proactive email and SMS delivery updates, building a consistent branded experience across the order-to-delivery journey, using AI-estimated delivery dates on product and checkout pages to reduce purchase hesitation, and automating returns and exchanges with options like store credit and product-based upsells to reclaim revenue that would otherwise be lost to refunds. AfterShip's pricing follows a freemium, shipment-volume-based model: a free plan covers roughly 50 shipments per month, and paid tiers scale up from an Essentials plan (roughly 11 dollars per month for a few hundred shipments) through Pro and Premium tiers (spanning roughly 100 to 250 dollars per month for one to three thousand shipments) up to custom Enterprise pricing for high-volume merchants who need dedicated account management, SLAs, and volume discounts. Each tier adds capability such as custom branding, advanced analytics, API access, and priority support, and overage fees apply per additional shipment beyond a plan's included volume, with a meaningful discount available for annual billing. AfterShip's main competitors in the shipment tracking and post-purchase space include Narvar, ParcelPanel, Track123, and Ship24, and it differentiates itself with the breadth of its post-purchase suite (tracking plus returns plus personalization) rather than tracking alone, along with its long operating history and deep Shopify ecosystem presence.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AfterShip
paragraphs: AfterShip was founded in 2012 to solve a simple ecommerce problem: shoppers constantly asking where is my order. It started as a shipment tracking API and has since grown into a full post-purchase suite used by online retailers to manage the entire delivery and returns experience under their own brand.

The company, now headquartered in Singapore after starting in Hong Kong, aggregates tracking data from more than 1,100 carriers worldwide and is widely used by Shopify and Shopify Plus merchants, alongside stores on WooCommerce, Magento, and BigCommerce.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AfterShip's flagship product is the branded tracking page, a customizable, multi-language page hosted on the merchant's own domain that shows real-time shipment status pulled from over a thousand carriers, paired with automated email and SMS delivery notifications.

Beyond tracking, AfterShip Returns automates the returns and exchanges process with self-service portals, store credit options, and AI-powered product recommendations designed to convert returns into exchanges. AI-estimated delivery dates can also be displayed on product and checkout pages before purchase to reduce cart abandonment caused by shipping uncertainty.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AfterShip uses a freemium, shipment-volume-based pricing model. A free plan covers a limited number of monthly shipments, and paid plans (Essentials, Pro, and Premium) scale up in both price and included shipment volume, with per-shipment overage fees beyond each plan's cap.

Enterprise customers with very high shipment volumes work directly with AfterShip's sales team for custom pricing that can include dedicated account management and service-level agreements. Annual billing is available at a discount compared to monthly billing.

cons: Costs scale quickly with shipment volume and overage fees

Enterprise pricing requires a sales conversation rather than transparent public listing
Configuring automation rules and notification templates has a learning curve

faqs.question: What does AfterShip cost?
faqs.answer: AfterShip offers a free plan for low shipment volumes, then paid plans starting around 11 dollars per month, scaling up based on monthly shipment count, plus custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: Does AfterShip integrate with Shopify?
faqs.answer: Yes, AfterShip has a native Shopify and Shopify Plus app for both its Tracking and Returns products.

faqs.question: What carriers does AfterShip support?
faqs.answer: AfterShip aggregates tracking data from more than 1,100 carriers worldwide.

faqs.question: Does AfterShip handle returns?
faqs.answer: Yes, AfterShip Returns is a dedicated product for self-service returns, exchanges, and store credit.

faqs.question: Is AfterShip only for Shopify stores?
faqs.answer: No, it also works with WooCommerce, Magento, BigCommerce, and custom stores via its API.

faqs.question: How is AfterShip different from a carrier's own tracking page?
faqs.answer: AfterShip unifies tracking data from many carriers into a single branded page on the merchant's own site, instead of sending customers to each carrier's separate site.

faqs.question: Does AfterShip charge per shipment?
faqs.answer: Yes, plans are based on monthly shipment volume with overage fees for shipments beyond a plan's included amount.

enrichment.short_description: Corel AfterShot Pro (originally Bibble) is a one-time-purchase RAW photo processing and batch workflow application for Windows, Mac, and Linux, now maintained by Alludo in a limited, low-frequency update cycle.
enrichment.headquarters: Ottawa, Ontario, Canada (Alludo, formerly Corel Corporation)
enrichment.seo_title: AfterShot Pro Review 2026: Still Worth Buying?
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AfterShot Pro review for 2026: history from Bibble to Corel, features, one-time pricing, and whether this RAW photo editor is still actively updated.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AfterShot (marketed as Corel AfterShot Pro) is a RAW photo processing and batch workflow application with a long and somewhat unusual history. The software began life as Bibble, created in 2000 by Eric Hyman's company Bibble Labs to process RAW files from Nikon's D1, and it became a well-regarded pioneer among third-party RAW converters, eventually supporting more than 70 camera models across Windows, Mac, and Linux. In late 2011 and early 2012, Corel Corporation acquired Bibble Labs and discontinued the Bibble brand, relaunching the software as Corel AfterShot Pro. Corel released AfterShot Pro 2 in 2014 and AfterShot Pro 3 in 2016, and the last publicly documented stable update, version 3.7.0.446, shipped in January 2021. As of 2026, that means the product has gone more than five years without a confirmed feature or compatibility update, even though it remains listed for sale on the official aftershotpro.com site (now operated under Corel's parent company, Alludo) at 79.99 dollars for a full license, with an upgrade price around 59.99 dollars for existing owners. This is important context for anyone researching the tool: it has not been formally discontinued or delisted, but its update cadence has slowed to what looks like maintenance-only status, which raises real questions about RAW support for cameras released after 2021 and about long-term compatibility with newer operating systems. AfterShot Pro should not be confused with AfterShip, a completely different company that builds ecommerce shipment tracking software; the two names are similar but the products serve entirely different markets. AfterShot Pro's core value proposition is a non-destructive, catalog-free batch RAW workflow: photographers point it at a folder of images rather than importing into a proprietary database, and can apply layers, noise reduction, HDR merging, and batch exports across large shoots quickly. It is sold as a one-time perpetual license rather than a subscription, which appeals to photographers who want to avoid recurring software costs, and it offers a rare native Linux build among commercial RAW converters, alongside Windows and Mac versions. It also supports third-party plugins such as the Perfect Photo Suite. Compared to modern subscription-based RAW editors like Adobe Lightroom or Capture One, AfterShot Pro is dramatically cheaper over time and works fully offline, but its interface, masking tools, and camera-support list look increasingly dated, and it lacks the AI-assisted editing features found in current-generation software. It is best suited to photographers who prioritize a lightweight, offline, subscription-free batch workflow over cutting-edge tools or guaranteed support for the newest camera bodies.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AfterShot Pro
paragraphs: AfterShot Pro traces its roots to Bibble, a RAW photo converter created in 2000 by Bibble Labs founder Eric Hyman to process files from Nikon's original D1 camera. Bibble grew into a well-regarded cross-platform RAW workflow tool before Corel Corporation acquired Bibble Labs around the end of 2011 and relaunched the product as Corel AfterShot Pro.

Corel (now operating under its parent company Alludo) has released three major versions since the rebrand, with AfterShot Pro 3 and its last documented update, version 3.7.0.446, dating to January 2021. It remains for sale as of 2026 but shows no confirmed recent development activity, so it is best understood as a mature product in a slow, maintenance-only phase rather than an actively evolving one. It is not related to AfterShip, the ecommerce shipment tracking company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AfterShot Pro is built around a non-destructive, folder-based RAW workflow: instead of importing photos into a proprietary catalog database, it works directly against folders on disk, which many photographers find faster and less fragile for large shoots.

The Pro tier adds layers, advanced noise reduction, and HDR merging on top of the base batch processing and export tools, and the software supports third-party plugins such as the Perfect Photo Suite. Notably, it offers a native Linux build, which is uncommon among commercial RAW converters that typically limit themselves to Windows and Mac.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AfterShot Pro is sold as a one-time perpetual license rather than a recurring subscription, priced at 79.99 dollars for a full new license, with a lower upgrade price of around 59.99 dollars for existing owners moving to a newer version.

A free, fully functional 30-day trial is available before purchase. Because there is no subscription, buyers pay once and can keep using the version they purchased indefinitely, though they will not receive new features or camera compatibility updates without buying a future paid upgrade, if one is ever released.

features.description: Applies consistent export settings and watermarks across large sets of images at once.

features.title: One-time perpetual license
features.description: Purchased once rather than billed on a recurring subscription basis.

features.title: Cross-platform availability
features.description: Runs on Windows, Mac, and Linux from a single product line.

Works fully offline with no cloud dependency
Rare native Linux support among RAW editors
Lightweight and fast for batch processing large photo shoots
No proprietary catalog database required, working directly against folders
cons: Last confirmed stable update was January 2021, so support for newer cameras is uncertain
Interface and masking tools look dated next to modern editors like Lightroom or Capture One
No mobile app or built-in cloud sync
Development pace has slowed dramatically, raising doubts about long-term viability
Limited AI-based editing tools compared to current-generation RAW software

faqs.question: Is AfterShot Pro still being sold?
faqs.answer: Yes, as of 2026 it remains listed for sale on the official aftershotpro.com site at 79.99 dollars for a full license.

faqs.question: Is AfterShot Pro still being actively updated?
faqs.answer: The last publicly documented stable update was version 3.7.0.446 in January 2021, so the product appears to be in a maintenance-only state with no confirmed recent development.

faqs.question: Is AfterShot Pro the same company as AfterShip?
faqs.answer: No, they are completely unrelated. AfterShip is an ecommerce shipment tracking company, while AfterShot Pro is desktop RAW photo editing software.

faqs.question: What cameras does AfterShot Pro support?
faqs.answer: It supports RAW formats from major camera manufacturers as of its last confirmed update in 2021; support for camera models released after that point is not confirmed.

faqs.question: Does AfterShot Pro require a subscription?
faqs.answer: No, it uses a one-time perpetual license model rather than a monthly or annual subscription.

faqs.question: What was AfterShot Pro before Corel acquired it?
faqs.answer: It began as Bibble, created by Bibble Labs (founded by Eric Hyman in 2000), before Corel acquired the company and rebranded the software.

faqs.question: Does AfterShot Pro work on Linux?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is one of the few commercial RAW converters that offers a native Linux version alongside Windows and Mac.

enrichment.short_description: AhaSend is a pay-as-you-go transactional email API and SMTP relay built on EU-based infrastructure, run by a small Amsterdam team focused on predictable deliverability and a permanent free tier.
enrichment.seo_title: AhaSend Review 2026: Pricing, Features and API
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AhaSend review for 2026 covering pricing, deliverability, EU data residency, and how this transactional email API compares to Postmark, SendGrid, and Resend.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AhaSend is a transactional email API and SMTP relay service built by AH Technology, a small company headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands, and founded in 2023. Its origin story is a common one in developer tools: the team behind it had previously built in-house email-sending infrastructure to solve their own frustration with slow, inconsistent delivery from other providers, and after running that infrastructure reliably at scale for their own products, they spun it out as a standalone commercial service. AhaSend runs on the KumoMTA mail transfer agent and operates a geo-redundant, EU-based infrastructure footprint, which appeals to companies that want GDPR-aligned data residency without adding extra legal or compliance layers required when routing email through US-based providers. The product is aimed squarely at developers sending transactional email such as password resets, receipts, order confirmations, and account notifications, rather than marketing or bulk email; the company deliberately avoids marketing-email features to keep its infrastructure and reputation focused on high-deliverability transactional traffic. AhaSend's pricing is pay-as-you-go with a genuinely permanent free tier of 1,000 emails per month, requiring no credit card to start, which the company positions against competitors that only offer time-limited trials. Paid usage begins around 10 dollars per month for approximately 25,000 emails, with additional volume billed at roughly 0.50 dollars per 1,000 emails and tiered discounts as usage grows; higher tiers (referred to as Pro and Max) add capabilities like dedicated IP addresses, longer message retention, OIDC single sign-on, and premium support, though exact pricing for the top Max tier is not published and requires checking AhaSend's current pricing page. Key features include a full management API for programmatic control of domains and sending, subaccounts that let agencies and SaaS platforms isolate sending environments for different clients or products under one parent account, SMTP relay support alongside the REST API, and delivery logs with configurable retention windows. AhaSend positions itself against established transactional email providers like Postmark, SendGrid, Resend, and Mailgun, arguing that its entry-level pricing undercuts Resend and matches SendGrid at comparable volume tiers, while its permanent free plan and EU-native infrastructure are differentiators few competitors match. The tradeoff is that AhaSend is a very young, very small company relative to those established players, so buyers evaluating it should weigh its lower cost and EU focus against a shorter track record and a smaller integration and support ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AhaSend
paragraphs: AhaSend is a transactional email API and SMTP relay service built by AH Technology, founded in 2023 and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. The product grew out of in-house email infrastructure the founding team built after struggling with delivery delays from other providers, later spun out as a standalone commercial service.

AhaSend focuses exclusively on transactional email, such as password resets, receipts, and account notifications, rather than marketing email, and it runs on EU-based infrastructure built on the KumoMTA mail transfer agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform provides a full management API and SMTP relay, subaccounts for agencies and platforms managing multiple senders, and dedicated IP addresses on paid tiers. Retention of message and delivery logs scales with plan level, from a few days on the free tier up to 30 days on higher tiers.

Because its infrastructure is EU-based, AhaSend appeals to companies that need GDPR-aligned data residency without routing email through US servers, and higher tiers add OIDC single sign-on and premium support for larger teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AhaSend offers a permanent free tier of 1,000 emails per month with no credit card required, followed by pay-as-you-go paid plans starting around 10 dollars per month for roughly 25,000 emails, with extra usage billed at about 0.50 dollars per 1,000 emails and volume discounts as usage scales.

Higher tiers, referred to as Pro and Max, add dedicated IPs, longer retention windows, and premium support, though the exact price of the top Max tier is not publicly listed and requires contacting AhaSend directly.

faqs.question: What is AhaSend?
faqs.answer: AhaSend is a pay-as-you-go transactional email API and SMTP relay service built on EU-based infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, AhaSend offers 1,000 emails per month free indefinitely, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: How does AhaSend pricing work?
faqs.answer: It is pay-as-you-go, with paid plans starting around 10 dollars per month for 25,000 emails and roughly 0.50 dollars per 1,000 additional emails, with tiered discounts at higher volumes.

faqs.question: Where is AhaSend's infrastructure located?
faqs.answer: AhaSend runs geo-redundant infrastructure based in the European Union.

faqs.question: Who is behind AhaSend?
faqs.answer: AhaSend is built by AH Technology, a company founded in 2023 and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands.

faqs.question: How does AhaSend compare to SendGrid or Resend?
faqs.answer: AhaSend offers lower entry-level pricing, a permanent free tier, and EU data residency, but it is a much smaller and younger company than established providers like SendGrid.

faqs.question: Does AhaSend support dedicated IPs?
faqs.answer: Yes, dedicated IP addresses are available starting on the paid Pro tier.

enrichment.short_description: Airbase, now operating as Airbase by Paylocity, is a spend management platform that unifies accounts payable automation, corporate cards, procurement, and expense management for mid-market companies.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California (acquired by Paylocity, headquartered in Schaumburg, Illinois, in 2024)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Airbase review for 2026: what the spend management platform does, its 2024 acquisition by Paylocity, key features, and how it compares to Ramp and Brex.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Airbase is a spend management platform founded in May 2017 by Thejo Kote, an entrepreneur who previously co-founded the connected-car company Automatic, which was acquired by SiriusXM for 115 million dollars. Kote started Airbase after experiencing firsthand how fragmented and manual spend management was at his previous startup, and he initially bootstrapped the company with around 300,000 dollars before raising a Series A from First Round Capital in 2019 and a 60 million dollar Series B led by Menlo Ventures in June 2021 at a roughly 600 million dollar post-money valuation, ultimately raising over 90 million dollars in total funding. Airbase built a unified procure-to-pay platform combining accounts payable automation, software-enabled corporate cards, employee expense management and reimbursements, and guided procurement with purchase-order workflows, all tied together with configurable approval policies and real-time spend visibility, plus deep integrations with ERPs like NetSuite. It processed roughly 3 billion dollars in payments annually for customers such as Cameo, Finix, Front, and Getaround before a major change in ownership: on October 1, 2024, HR and payroll company Paylocity completed its acquisition of Airbase for approximately 325 million dollars. As of 2026, the product operates under the branding Airbase by Paylocity, integrated into what Paylocity markets as Paylocity for Finance, combining Airbase's non-payroll spend management with Paylocity's payroll and human capital management platform so customers can see both payroll and non-payroll spend in one system. Airbase deliberately positioned itself differently from self-serve rivals like Ramp and Brex: where Ramp and Brex historically offered free or low-cost card products monetized primarily through interchange revenue and targeted broad startup and SMB audiences, Airbase targeted mid-market companies (roughly 100 or more employees) with a paid, software-subscription-first model, with an estimated 70 to 80 percent or more of its revenue coming from SaaS subscriptions rather than card interchange. Airbase does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers must request a demo and receive a custom quote based on company size and modules needed. Its main competitors remain Ramp, Brex, and BILL, and its key differentiator continues to be the breadth of its unified platform (procurement, AP, cards, and expenses in one system with strong approval workflows) now paired with the reach and resources of its Paylocity parent company.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Airbase
paragraphs: Airbase is a spend management platform founded in May 2017 by Thejo Kote, who previously built and sold the connected-car startup Automatic to SiriusXM. Airbase combines accounts payable automation, corporate cards, employee expense management, and guided procurement into a single system built for mid-market companies.

After bootstrapping the company and later raising a Series A from First Round Capital and a Series B led by Menlo Ventures, Airbase was acquired by HR and payroll company Paylocity in a deal that closed October 1, 2024 for roughly 325 million dollars. It now operates as Airbase by Paylocity.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Airbase's platform centers on accounts payable automation and bill pay, software-enabled corporate cards with configurable spend controls, and guided procurement with purchase-order workflows, all governed by approval policies that can be customized by department and spend category.

It also includes employee expense management and reimbursements, real-time budgeting and spend dashboards, and deep integrations with ERP systems such as NetSuite. Since the Paylocity acquisition, Airbase capabilities are increasingly bundled with Paylocity's payroll and HCM platform under the Paylocity for Finance branding, giving customers combined visibility into payroll and non-payroll spend.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Airbase does not publish self-serve pricing tiers. Historically, its revenue model combined SaaS subscription fees (an estimated 70 to 80 percent or more of revenue) with corporate card interchange revenue, differentiating it from competitors that leaned more heavily on free, interchange-funded products.

Prospective customers must request a demo and receive a custom quote based on company size, transaction volume, and which modules (AP, cards, procurement, expenses) they need, a structure that has continued following the Paylocity acquisition.

faqs.answer: Paylocity acquired Airbase for approximately 325 million dollars, with the deal completing on October 1, 2024.

enrichment.short_description: Airware was a commercial drone software and hardware company founded in 2011 that built an enterprise aerial data platform before shutting down in September 2018 after raising and spending 118 million dollars in venture funding.
enrichment.company_size: up to approx 140 employees at its peak, before its 2018 shutdown
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California (originally founded in Newport Beach, California)
enrichment.seo_title: Airware: What Happened to the Drone Startup (2026)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Airware is a defunct drone software company that shut down in September 2018 after raising 118 million dollars. Here is its history, product, and collapse.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Airware is a defunct company, not an active software product, and anyone researching it in 2026 should understand it as a historical case study rather than a tool currently available for purchase or use. It was founded in 2011 in Newport Beach, California, by Jonathan Downey, an MIT-trained electrical engineer and computer scientist who had previously built autonomous flight-control software for Boeing and organized an MIT student drone team. Airware relocated to San Francisco in January 2014 and grew into one of the best-funded companies in the commercial drone industry, eventually raising approximately 118 million dollars in venture capital from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, Google's GV, Kleiner Perkins, and Intel Capital, and adding former Cisco CEO John Chambers to its board. Airware's product combined autopilot hardware and on-aircraft flight-control software with a cloud-based aerial data platform, aimed at enterprise customers in industries like construction, mining, agriculture, and insurance who wanted to collect and analyze drone-captured data at scale, positioning itself as an operating system for commercial drones rather than a drone manufacturer itself. The business model shifted over time from selling autopilot hardware toward an enterprise software and data-analytics platform, but the company struggled to keep pace with the plummeting cost and rapidly improving quality of drone hardware from vertically integrated manufacturers, especially China's DJI, whose bundled flight software increasingly made third-party autopilot systems like Airware's unnecessary for many customers. After a period of declining relevance and multiple rounds of layoffs over roughly two years, Airware abruptly informed its remaining employees, at that point around 140 people, that it would cease all operations immediately, which it did on September 14, 2018. In the aftermath, Airware's assets, including some intellectual property and select team members, were acquired by Delair, a French drone manufacturer and asset-management company based in Toulouse. There is no current product, pricing, support, or company to evaluate; Airware exists today only as a well-documented example of a heavily funded hardware-and-software startup that could not survive the commoditization of its core market. This entry is included for historical accuracy and research purposes, and should not be mistaken for information about an active, purchasable tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Airware Was
paragraphs: Airware was a commercial drone software and hardware company founded in 2011 in Newport Beach, California, by Jonathan Downey, and later relocated to San Francisco in January 2014. It built autopilot systems and an enterprise aerial data platform aimed at industries like construction, mining, and agriculture.

The company raised approximately 118 million dollars in venture funding from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, GV, Kleiner Perkins, and Intel Capital, and it was widely regarded as one of the best-capitalized companies in the early commercial drone industry.

long_form_blocks.heading: Why Airware Shut Down
paragraphs: Airware's business model shifted over time from selling proprietary autopilot hardware toward enterprise aerial-data software, but it struggled to compete as drone hardware makers, particularly China's DJI, drove down costs and improved bundled flight software to the point where third-party autopilot systems became less necessary for many customers.

After roughly two years of declining relevance and repeated layoffs, Airware ceased all operations on September 14, 2018, informing its remaining approximately 140 employees the business was shutting down immediately. Its assets were later acquired by Delair, a French drone and asset-management company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Current Status
paragraphs: Airware no longer exists as an operating company, and it has no current product, pricing, or support available. Anyone encountering the Airware name today, including on its former website, should treat it as a historical reference rather than an active software tool.

faqs.question: What happened to Airware?
faqs.answer: It ran out of funding after struggling to compete against cheaper, vertically integrated drone hardware from companies like DJI, and was forced to shut down.

faqs.answer: Jonathan Downey founded Airware in 2011 in Newport Beach, California.

faqs.answer: Approximately 118 million dollars from investors including Andreessen Horowitz, GV, and Kleiner Perkins.

faqs.answer: Delair, a French drone and asset-management company, acquired Airware's assets and some team members in 2018.

faqs.answer: No, the product is no longer sold, supported, or maintained by any company.

enrichment.short_description: AISEO is an AI-powered content and SEO platform that has expanded from AI writing and paraphrasing tools into technical SEO audits and generative engine optimization tracking of brand visibility across AI chat tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.
enrichment.seo_title: AISEO Review 2026: AI Writing and SEO Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AISEO review for 2026 covering AI content generation, SEO audits, AI-search brand tracking, pricing tiers, and how it fits alongside dedicated SEO tools.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AISEO is an AI-powered content and search optimization platform operated by a company founded in 2021 and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. It began primarily as an AI writing and paraphrasing tool aimed at bloggers, students, marketers, and small business owners who needed SEO-friendly blog posts and articles generated quickly, with features like a Longform Assistant, a one-click blog generator, readability scoring, and an AI humanizer designed to make generated text read more naturally and, according to the company's own marketing, to pass detection tools like GPTZero and Turnitin. Over time the platform has broadened considerably beyond pure content generation into a combined SEO and generative engine optimization (GEO) product. Its current feature set includes an SEO Agent that crawls a website daily and flags more than 50 potential ranking issues prioritized by impact, a Brand Monitor that tracks how and how often a brand is mentioned or recommended inside AI-generated answers from tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, Copilot, and Perplexity, and a Reddit Agent that monitors brand mentions in Reddit threads that rank on Google, reflecting the growing importance of Reddit content in search results. Supporting tools include keyword research, competitor and backlink analysis, automatic schema markup generation, and an advanced paraphraser for adjusting tone and fluency. AISEO's pricing has shifted across different marketing pages found during research, which suggests the company revises its plans relatively often; the most recent official pricing page shows an Entry plan around 39 dollars per month (after a low-cost first-period trial, often around 1 dollar) covering one tracked website and one monitored brand, and a Pro plan around 119 dollars per month covering three websites and three brands with a fuller SEO Agent, competitor tracking, backlink analysis, and a monthly AI credit allowance. Earlier pricing pages referenced lower-cost Grow and Scale plans oriented more around word-based content generation credits, suggesting the product's packaging has evolved alongside its feature set. AISEO is best understood as a hybrid tool sitting between AI writing assistants, technical SEO auditors, and the newer category of AI-search visibility trackers, rather than a single-purpose competitor to any one specialist tool like Jasper (writing), Ahrefs or Semrush (SEO), or dedicated GEO-monitoring startups. Its appeal is breadth and a relatively low entry price, while the tradeoff is less category-specific depth than tools built to do just one of those jobs.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AISEO
paragraphs: AISEO is a content and search optimization platform from a company founded in 2021 and headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands. It started as an AI writing and paraphrasing tool for SEO-friendly blog content and has since expanded into technical SEO auditing and AI-search visibility tracking.

The platform now spans three overlapping areas: AI content generation, traditional on-site SEO auditing, and generative engine optimization (GEO), which monitors how a brand appears in answers from AI chat tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The SEO Agent crawls a website daily and surfaces more than 50 potential ranking issues prioritized by likely impact, while the Brand Monitor tracks brand mentions and sentiment inside AI-generated answers across major AI assistants, and the Reddit Agent watches brand mentions in Reddit threads that rank in Google search results.

On the content side, AISEO offers a Longform Assistant, an AI Article Generator, an advanced paraphraser, an AI humanizer, keyword research, competitor and backlink analysis, and automatic schema markup and image generation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AISEO's most recent official pricing shows an Entry plan around 39 dollars per month, typically preceded by a short, low-cost trial period, covering one tracked website and one monitored brand with basic SEO issue detection. A Pro plan around 119 dollars per month covers three websites and three brands with a fuller SEO Agent, competitor tracking, backlinks analysis, and a monthly AI credit allowance.

AISEO's pricing structure has changed noticeably across different points in the company's history, moving from word-count-based content generation credits toward a model built more around websites tracked and brands monitored, so buyers should confirm current pricing directly on aiseo.ai before committing.

faqs.question: What does AISEO do?
faqs.answer: It combines AI content generation with technical SEO auditing and AI-search (GEO) brand visibility tracking across tools like ChatGPT and Gemini.

faqs.question: How much does AISEO cost?
faqs.answer: Current published pricing starts around 39 dollars per month for the Entry plan, with a Pro plan around 119 dollars per month, though pricing has changed over time and should be confirmed on aiseo.ai.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial?
faqs.answer: AISEO typically offers a short, low-cost trial period, often around 1 dollar for the first billing period, before regular pricing applies.

faqs.question: What is GEO tracking in AISEO?
faqs.answer: It monitors how often and how favorably a brand is mentioned in AI-generated answers from tools like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude.

faqs.question: Can AISEO help bypass AI content detectors?
faqs.answer: The company markets an AI Humanizer feature intended to make generated text read as more human-written, though effectiveness against detection tools can vary.

faqs.question: Who founded AISEO?
faqs.answer: The company was founded in 2021 and is headquartered in Amsterdam, Netherlands.

faqs.question: Does AISEO replace a dedicated SEO tool like Ahrefs or Semrush?
faqs.answer: It offers overlapping features such as keyword research and backlink analysis, but it is not as specialized or deep as dedicated SEO suites.

enrichment.short_description: AKQA is a global digital design, innovation, and advertising agency founded in 1994 by Ajaz Ahmed, now operating as part of WPP's AKQA Group alongside Grey, serving major brands on custom, project-based engagements rather than self-serve software.
enrichment.company_size: approx 6,000 employees across AKQA Group, following its 2020 merger with Grey Group
enrichment.headquarters: London, United Kingdom (global network of offices)
enrichment.seo_title: AKQA Review 2026: Global Digital Agency Profile
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AKQA profile for 2026: history since 1994, WPP ownership, the AKQA Group merger with Grey, notable clients, and how its custom, project-based pricing works.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AKQA is not a self-serve software tool but a global digital design, innovation, and advertising agency, and it is important to evaluate it on those terms rather than as SaaS. It was founded in London in 1994 by Ajaz Ahmed, who created the agency at age 21 and named it after his own initials. AKQA expanded internationally in 2001 through a merger with agencies based in San Francisco, Washington D.C., and Singapore, growing from a UK digital shop into a multinational network that attracted early blue-chip clients including Virgin, Microsoft, McDonald's, BMW, and Coca-Cola. In 2012, global marketing holding company WPP acquired a majority stake in AKQA, buying out growth-equity investor General Atlantic and part of management's remaining shares, in a deal that valued the company at roughly 540 million dollars; AKQA has operated as a WPP subsidiary ever since while retaining its own brand identity. In November 2020, WPP restructured its agency portfolio by merging AKQA with Grey Group, forming AKQA Group as an umbrella organization that keeps AKQA and Grey as distinct client-facing brands under shared leadership and infrastructure; the combined organization employs roughly 6,000 people across about 50 countries. A significant leadership change occurred in October 2024, when founder Ajaz Ahmed stepped down as CEO of AKQA Group after 30 years running the company he started. Today, AKQA's client roster reportedly includes more than half of the Fortune 500's top 20 companies, spanning recent work with brands like Nike, BBC Earth, and H&M, and its service lines cover experience design, product and ecommerce development, CRM and customer experience consulting, brand strategy and communications, and technology and innovation consulting, including emerging areas like AI and immersive experiences. Because AKQA is a full-service agency rather than a productized tool, it has no published pricing, plans, or tiers of the kind found on this directory for SaaS products; engagements are scoped and priced individually per client, typically as project fees or ongoing retainers negotiated with each brand based on scope, team size, and duration. This makes AKQA fundamentally different from most entries in a software directory: prospective clients contact the agency directly to discuss a brief and receive a custom proposal, rather than signing up for a subscription or self-service plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AKQA
paragraphs: AKQA is a global digital design and advertising agency founded in London in 1994 by Ajaz Ahmed, who named the company after his own initials at age 21. It expanded internationally in 2001 through a merger with agencies in San Francisco, Washington D.C., and Singapore, growing into a multinational network serving major global brands.

AKQA is not a self-serve software product. It is a service business that WPP, one of the world's largest marketing holding companies, acquired a majority stake in during 2012, and later merged with Grey Group in November 2020 to form AKQA Group, an umbrella organization employing roughly 6,000 people worldwide.

long_form_blocks.heading: Agency Capabilities
paragraphs: AKQA's service lines span digital product design and development, ecommerce experience design and build, CRM and customer experience consulting, brand strategy and communications campaigns, and broader technology and platform engineering work for enterprise clients.

The agency also runs innovation and emerging-technology labs exploring areas like AI and immersive experiences, and it works with a client roster that reportedly includes more than half of the Fortune 500's top 20 companies, alongside brands such as Nike, BBC Earth, and H&M.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AKQA does not publish pricing plans or tiers because it operates as a project-based and retainer-based agency rather than a productized software tool. Each engagement is scoped and priced individually based on the client's brief, required team size, and project duration.

Prospective clients typically contact AKQA directly to discuss a project or retainer and receive a custom proposal, a structure common among large creative and consulting agencies but very different from the self-serve pricing found on most SaaS tools in this directory.

faqs.question: Is AKQA an independent company?
